mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Peru on?
mapQuestions.answer: South America. Peru sits on the west coast of the continent, facing the Pacific Ocean, with Ecuador and Colombia to the north and Brazil and Bolivia to the east 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Peru?
mapQuestions.answer: Lima, on the desert Pacific coast. About one in three Peruvians lives in or around Lima, and it is the second-driest capital city in the world after Cairo.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Peru speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Spanish is the main one, but Quechua and Aymara are also official languages. Quechua was the language of the Inca Empire and is still spoken by around 4 million people in the highlands today.

mapQuestions.question: Which long mountain range runs down the spine of Peru?
mapQuestions.answer: The Andes. They are the longest mountain range on land, stretching 7,000 km down South America. About a third of Peru is high Andean plateau and snowy peaks.

mapQuestions.question: Which famous Inca city sits high in the Peruvian Andes?
mapQuestions.answer: Machu Picchu, built around 1450 on a ridge 2,430 metres above sea level. The outside world did not learn about it until 1911, when explorer Hiram Bingham was led there by a local farmer.

foods.name: Ceviche
foods.description: Fresh raw white fish cured in lime juice, mixed with red onion, chilli, coriander, sweet potato and crunchy giant Andean corn. Peru's national dish, eaten at lunchtime.

foods.name: Lomo saltado
foods.description: Beef stir-fried with onion, tomato, soy sauce and chips, served on a bed of rice. A delicious mash-up of Chinese cooking and Andean ingredients, invented by Chinese immigrants in Lima.

foods.name: Ají de gallina
foods.description: Shredded chicken in a creamy yellow chilli sauce thickened with bread, walnuts and milk. Served with boiled potato, rice, olives and a hard-boiled egg.

foods.name: Causa rellena
foods.description: Cold layered terrine of mashed yellow potato, lime and chilli, stuffed with tuna, chicken or avocado. Peru has over 4,000 different varieties of potato!

foods.name: Anticuchos
foods.description: Marinated meat skewers grilled over charcoal at street stalls in the evening. Traditionally beef heart (yes, really, and it is delicious), but also chicken.

foods.name: Picarones
foods.description: Sweet pumpkin and sweet-potato doughnut rings, deep-fried and drizzled with chancaca (a thick spiced sugar-cane syrup). The classic Peruvian dessert.

scavengerItems.label: A real llama or alpaca (they really are everywhere in the highlands)
Bright handwoven textiles with Andean geometric patterns
A market stall with 10+ different varieties of potato
A woman in traditional Andean dress with a tall bowler hat or felt hat
Someone playing pan flutes or a charango (small Andean string instrument)
The Andes mountains with snow on top
A sol coin with the Inca sun on the back
A bright "combi" minibus with the conductor shouting destinations out of the door
A real toucan, scarlet macaw, or bright parrot (Amazon side)
A purple drink (chicha morada, made from purple corn, totally non-alcoholic and amazing)
An Inca stone wall fitted so tight you cannot slide paper through (no mortar at all)
The dry coastal Pacific desert (Peru's coast is one of the driest places on Earth)

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the desert Pacific coast. Colonial centre, modern Miraflores cliff-top promenades, and one of the best food scenes in the world.
The old Inca capital, 3,400 m up in the Andes. Spanish colonial buildings sit on top of perfectly cut Inca stone walls that have survived every earthquake since.
The cloud-shrouded Inca city on a ridge above the Urubamba river, built around 1450 and abandoned about a century later. One of the New 7 Wonders.
The highest navigable lake in the world (3,812 m), shared with Bolivia. Home to the floating reed islands of the Uros people, who have lived on woven totora reeds for centuries.
The largest city in the world you cannot reach by road, deep in Peru's Amazon. You can only fly in or arrive by boat up the river.
Giant 2,000-year-old line drawings in the southern desert. Monkeys, hummingbirds, spiders, a 96-metre "astronaut", only fully visible from the air.

senses.smell: Roast corn on a street grill, eucalyptus leaves in highland villages, salty Pacific air in Lima, fresh coriander on ceviche, woodsmoke from an Andean kitchen…
senses.hear: Pan flutes echoing across mountain valleys, Spanish mixed with Quechua words, "¡Qué rico!", combi conductors shouting "Sube, sube!" (get on, get on), llamas humming softly, market vendors singing prices…
senses.taste: Tangy lime-cured fish with crunchy Andean corn, four different potato varieties on one plate, smoky grilled meat skewers, syrupy picarones, sweet purple chicha morada…
senses.feel: Thin Andean air that makes you breathless on your first day, the bumpy seat of a bus winding up a mountain pass, scratchy alpaca wool, cool Inca stone polished smooth by 600 years of hands, hot desert sun on the coast…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How was a "lost" city found again?
stories.body: In 1911 an American history teacher called Hiram Bingham came to Peru looking for the legendary last Inca city. A local farmer called Melchor Arteaga led him through thick jungle to a ridge 2,430 metres up. Hiding under all the vines were the perfectly cut stone walls of a whole Inca city, abandoned for nearly 400 years. The local people had always known it was there, of course, but the outside world had not. Today Machu Picchu is one of the New 7 Wonders of the World and over a million people visit each year. The numbers are now limited to protect the site from being walked to pieces.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole city sat unnoticed under jungle for centuries because nobody from far away had written it down. What other things might be "known" by locals but "lost" to the rest of the world right now?

stories.question: How big was the Inca Empire, really?
stories.body: At its peak, the Inca Empire stretched 4,000 kilometres along the Andes, from southern Colombia all the way down to central Chile. It was the largest empire in the Americas before the Europeans arrived, larger than the Roman Empire at its peak. And the Inca did all this in only about 100 years. They built more than 40,000 km of stone roads, with rope bridges across canyons, mountain tunnels, and runners called "chasquis" who could relay a message 240 km in a single day. They had no horses, no iron, and no wheels. They did not even have a written language, they kept records using knotted strings called "quipus".

stories.question: How many kinds of potato are there?
stories.body: Peru is the birthplace of the potato. Andean farmers first started growing them about 8,000 years ago, near Lake Titicaca. Today Peru grows over 4,000 different varieties: yellow ones, purple ones, blue ones, red ones, knobbly ones the size of a marble, fat ones the size of your head. Some are sweet, some are floury, some are waxy. Peruvians have a different potato for every recipe, and they freeze-dry some of them up on the cold mountain tops to store for the winter. When Spanish ships finally took the potato to Europe in the 1500s, it changed the world: nearly every potato dish you have ever eaten started here.

stories.question: Who drew the Nazca Lines, and why?
stories.body: In the desert south of Lima are huge line drawings scratched into the ground: a 96-metre human figure, a 50-metre monkey with a curly tail, a hummingbird, a spider, geometric trapezoids, all only visible properly from the sky. The Nazca people drew them between about 500 BC and 500 AD, by clearing away the dark surface stones to show the lighter sand underneath. Nobody is completely sure why. Theories include religious paths walked in ceremony, water-finding maps, or messages to the gods. They have survived 2,000 years because the desert almost never rains. Now planes do small flights over them so visitors can see them properly.

stories.question: How did a few hundred Spaniards conquer an empire of millions?
stories.body: In 1532 the Spanish soldier Francisco Pizarro arrived in northern Peru with just 168 men and 62 horses. He met the Inca emperor Atahualpa at Cajamarca. The Inca had no idea what horses were, had never seen a gun fired, and were already weakened by smallpox (a disease the Europeans had brought to the Americas which had killed millions before the Spanish ever met the Inca in person). The Spanish captured Atahualpa, demanded a room filled with gold and another with silver as ransom, then killed him anyway. Within a few years, the entire empire had fallen. It is a hard story, and a lot of Peruvian identity today comes from holding both halves: deep Inca pride and Spanish colonial inheritance, in one country.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Diseases the Spanish accidentally carried killed more people than their guns did. How much of history do you think was decided by tiny things like that?

convoQuestions.question: Highlight food of Peru so far?
Have you tried saying "gracias" or "qué rico" yet? What happened?
Mountain, desert, jungle or coast, which has felt the most "Peru" so far?
If we could pick ONE Inca thing to take home with us, what would it be?
Peru has over 4,000 kinds of potato. How many have you eaten this trip?
The Inca built a whole empire without writing or the wheel. How do you think they managed it?

wordSearch.hint: Peruvian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Llama
animals.description: Tall, woolly, found everywhere in the highlands. Used for carrying loads up Andean trails for thousands of years.

animals.name: Alpaca
animals.description: Smaller and fluffier than a llama, with a much softer fleece. The high-altitude grasslands of Puno and Cusco are full of them.

animals.name: Inca tern
animals.description: Striking grey seabird with a curling white moustache, on the Pacific cliffs of the Paracas peninsula.

animals.name: Andean condor
animals.description: One of the biggest flying birds on Earth (3 m wingspan). Best seen at Colca Canyon at sunrise.

animals.name: Pink river dolphin
animals.description: In the Peruvian Amazon, around Iquitos. Yes, actually pink.

animals.name: Vicuña
animals.description: The wild ancestor of the alpaca, with the finest wool in the world. Protected, in the high Andean plains.
